definition: A privacy-enabled interoperable blockchain network designed for institutional and decentralized finance, built on Daml smart contracts and synchronized via the Global Synchronizer.
The native utility token of the Canton Network, used for transaction fees, staking, validator rewards, and governance participation.
Canton BTC — a Bitcoin-backed token on Canton Network issued by BitSafe, enabling BTC holders to participate in Canton DeFi without selling their Bitcoin.
A purpose-built smart contract language developed by Digital Asset, designed for privacy-preserving multi-party workflows with built-in authorization controls.
A component in Canton that sequences and orders transactions atomically across multiple participants, ensuring consistency without revealing private data to non-involved parties.
The shared, network-wide synchronizer for Canton that enables cross-application atomic transactions and serves as the backbone for CC transfers and staking.
A node operator on Canton Network that validates transactions, participates in consensus, and earns CC rewards for securing the network.
A top-tier validator on Canton Network with additional governance rights and higher staking requirements, responsible for critical network operations on the Global Synchronizer.
A decentralized party management and identity service on Canton enabling governance participation, validator coordination, and on-chain identity resolution.
The non-profit organization governing the Canton Network, overseeing the Global Synchronizer, CC distribution, and ecosystem grant programs.
The formal process for proposing changes to the Canton Network protocol, analogous to EIPs on Ethereum. CIPs go through community review before implementation.
The throughput capacity allocated to participants on the Global Synchronizer, measured in transaction units. Validators purchase bandwidth with CC to process transactions.
The cost paid in CC to process a transaction on Canton Network. Fees compensate validators and synchronizer operators for bandwidth and computational resources.
A core design principle of Canton where transaction details are only visible to directly involved parties, unlike public blockchains where all data is globally visible.
A component of a Canton transaction that is only visible to the parties directly involved, enabling privacy-preserving multi-step workflows across participants.
The process by which a Canton transaction is validated and committed by all required parties and the synchronizer, achieving finality.
A logical identity on Canton Network that can own contracts, submit transactions, and observe state. Parties are the core unit of authorization in Daml.
A node in the Canton Network that hosts parties, stores their contract state, and submits transactions to synchronizers on their behalf.
The process of locking CC tokens with a validator to help secure the Canton Network and earn proportional rewards from transaction fees and protocol emissions.
CC tokens earned by validators and their delegators for participating in transaction validation and network consensus on Canton.
The act of assigning your CC stake to a validator without transferring custody, allowing passive participation in network security and reward earning.
The percentage of staking rewards that a validator retains as compensation for operating infrastructure, with the remainder distributed to delegators.
Self-executing code deployed on a blockchain that enforces agreed-upon rules between parties. On Canton, smart contracts are written in Daml.
An atomic state change on the Canton Network consisting of one or more sub-transactions that create, archive, or exercise choices on Daml contracts.
The process of representing real-world assets (securities, commodities, currencies) as digital tokens on a blockchain like Canton Network.
The Canton Network representation of Circle's USDC stablecoin, providing dollar-pegged liquidity for DeFi protocols and trading on Canton.
A cryptocurrency designed to maintain a stable value relative to a reference asset, typically the US dollar. USDCx and USYC are examples on Canton.
US Yield Coin by Hashnote — a tokenized short-duration US Treasury product on Canton that provides institutional-grade yield while maintaining on-chain liquidity.
Financial services built on blockchain technology that operate without centralized intermediaries. Canton DeFi includes DEXs, lending protocols, and yield products.
A peer-to-peer marketplace for trading tokens without a central authority. Temple and Denex are Canton DEXs.
A DEX mechanism that uses mathematical formulas and liquidity pools instead of order books to determine asset prices and enable trades.
A smart contract holding paired token reserves that enables automated trading. Liquidity providers deposit tokens and earn fees from trades.
A user who deposits tokens into a liquidity pool to facilitate trading, earning a share of trading fees proportional to their contribution.
A DeFi protocol that allows users to lend assets to earn interest or borrow assets against collateral. Alpend is Canton's lending protocol.
Assets locked in a smart contract to secure a loan or position. On Canton, CC and CBTC can serve as collateral for borrowing on protocols like Alpend.
The automatic sale of collateral when a borrower's position falls below the required collateral ratio, protecting lenders from losses.
The return earned on deposited or staked assets, expressed as an annual percentage. Sources include staking rewards, lending interest, and LP fees.
The decision-making process for protocol changes on Canton, including CIP proposals, validator voting, and Canton Foundation oversight.
A web application for searching and viewing blockchain data including transactions, addresses, and network statistics. Cantonscan and CC View serve Canton.
Software that manages private keys and enables users to send, receive, and store digital assets. Loop Wallet and Bron are popular Canton wallets.
A model where users maintain sole control of their private keys and assets, without relying on a third-party custodian.
A secret cryptographic key that proves ownership of assets and authorizes transactions. Must never be shared or exposed.
A measure of blockchain throughput indicating how many transactions the network can process each second.
A fixed time period in the Canton protocol during which validator sets and reward distributions are calculated.
The guarantee that a confirmed transaction cannot be reversed or altered. Canton achieves fast finality through its synchronizer-based consensus.
The ability for different blockchain applications and networks to communicate and transact with each other. Canton achieves this via its synchronizer architecture.
A transaction that either completes entirely or fails entirely, with no partial execution. Canton's synchronizer ensures atomicity across multiple applications.
The protocol layer within Splice that manages Canton Coin issuance, reward distribution, and network fee mechanics.
The infrastructure platform providing the Global Synchronizer, wallet kernel (SGK), and amulet protocol that form the operational backbone of Canton Network.
A developer SDK by Splice/Five North that provides core wallet functionality for building Canton-compatible wallet applications.
Canton's naming service that maps human-readable names to party identifiers, similar to ENS on Ethereum.
The technology company that created Daml and Canton, led by CEO Yuval Rooz, focused on institutional-grade blockchain infrastructure.
The distribution of control and decision-making across a network of participants rather than a single central authority.
The total value of a cryptocurrency calculated by multiplying the current price by the circulating supply. A key metric for comparing asset sizes.
The number of tokens currently available and circulating in the market, excluding locked, burned, or reserved tokens.
The maximum number of tokens that will ever exist for a given cryptocurrency, including those not yet in circulation.
The economic model and incentive structure of a cryptocurrency, including supply schedules, distribution, utility, and fee mechanisms.
A unit measuring the computational effort required to execute transactions on a blockchain. Canton uses bandwidth-based fee models instead of traditional gas.
